data.title: Remitly outlines 2026 revenue guidance of up to $1.96B as new products and AI boost expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Remitly Global, Inc. (RELY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Matthew Oppenheimer, CEO, opened the call by announcing the appointment of Sebastian Gunningham as Remitly's new CEO, emphasizing that "We ended this strategically important year with incredible results, growing revenue by 29% and reaching adjusted EBITDA of $272 million in 2025, exceeding our guidance for both." Oppenheimer highlighted the company's transformation over five years, noting "We are now over $1.6 billion in revenue, serving more than 9 million customers." He stated that the company's core money movement business continues to show strong momentum, with less than 4% market share in the consumer total addressable market, providing significant headroom for expansion. Oppenheimer cited the growth of new products, such as Flex (Send Now, Pay Later), which reached around 120,000 users, and Remitly Business, which ended the year with over 15,000 business customers. He also outlined strategic priorities for 2026, including further expansion in high-amount senders and geographical growth in regions like the UAE, Japan, Saudi Arabia, and potentially Brazil.

  • Sebastian Gunningham, the incoming CEO, introduced himself and described his background in product, engineering, and financial services, noting, “Remitly serves a global community that has been historically underserved and overcharged.” Gunningham stressed his focus on building the best product, leveraging AI, and driving growth: “In digital financial services, the product is the business. That will be my focus and the way we keep growing our customer base, delivering a great service and driving our revenue growth.”

  • Vikas Mehta, CFO, stated, “Fourth quarter was $442 million revenue, up 26% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA was $89 million, resulting in an adjusted EBITDA margin of 20%, our highest quarterly adjusted EBITDA margin ever.” Mehta emphasized efficiency gains, record low transaction losses, and disciplined expense management as key drivers for profitability.

Outlook

  • The company expects Q1 2026 revenue of $436 million to $438 million, reflecting 21% growth. For the full year 2026, revenue guidance is between $1.94 billion and $1.96 billion, or 19% to 20% growth. Mehta said, “We expect revenue from new products to more than double in 2026.” Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 is projected between $82 million and $84 million, with a margin of around 19%. For the full year, adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $340 million and $360 million, with a margin of around 18%. The company expects to generate positive GAAP net income each quarter and strong year-over-year growth in GAAP net income and free cash flow.

Financial Results

  • Remitly reported Q4 revenue of $442 million, up 26% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA of $89 million, with a 20% margin. Full year revenue was $1.635 billion, up 29%, and adjusted EBITDA reached $272 million, with a 17% margin. Net income for the year was $68 million, with $41 million in Q4 alone. Send volume for Q4 grew 35% to $21 billion, and send volume per active customer increased 13% year-over-year. Quarterly active customers rose 19% to nearly 9.3 million. Growth in high and very high amount senders was notable, with high amount sender volume up 14% and very high amount sender volume up 105% year-over-year.

  • U.S. revenue grew 28%, while Rest of World revenue increased 26%. Revenue from transactions outside India, the Philippines, and Mexico now comprises over half of the revenue mix.

  • New products contributed a little more than 1% to total revenue in 2025; this figure is expected to more than double in 2026.

  • Free cash flow for 2025 was $283 million, more than tripling from the prior year.

Q&A

  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan: Asked Gunningham how his prior experience prepares him for Remitly. Gunningham replied, "Some of my prior experiences include leading product organizations and engineering organizations in some of the best companies in the world. Some of those were big, some of those were smaller. I've run large complex businesses in many continents. I've been a CEO a number of times."

  • Ramsey El-Assal, Cantor Fitzgerald: Questioned macro impacts like FX or immigration policy. Mehta responded, “Our customer is very resilient even in sort of geopolitical volatile background, which again strengthens our confidence with regards to the guidance.”

  • Aditya Buddhavarapu, BofA Securities: Inquired about revenue cadence and margin outlook for 2026. Mehta explained that Q1 benefits from the 1% remittance tax and that margin guidance for the full year is balanced between growth investments and ongoing efficiency.

  • William Nance, Goldman Sachs: Asked Gunningham about areas of focus as CEO. Gunningham emphasized product development velocity and addressing large markets.

  • Cristopher Kennedy, William Blair: Asked about AI opportunities. Gunningham replied, “There are a number of buckets of AI... the right AI adoption just gives us a lot of tailwinds into the next few years.”

  • Alexander Markgraff, KeyBanc: Asked about benefits from Flex and Remitly One. Mehta highlighted strong unit economics and higher spending among Flex users.

  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe Research: Asked about high amount senders. Mehta reported “north of 40%” growth for high amount senders and “north of 100%” growth for very high amount senders.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed appreciation for Oppenheimer’s leadership, welcomed Gunningham, and raised questions centered on growth drivers, macro impacts, and new products. The tone was slightly positive, with curiosity about sustainability and scalability.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks, using phrases like “I could not be more excited” and “We remain focused on long-term RLTE dollar growth.” In the Q&A, responses were measured, providing detail but deferring on specifics where appropriate, especially from Gunningham as new CEO.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more upbeat due to record results and a smooth leadership transition. Analysts were slightly more focused on execution risks and guidance details than before.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth accelerated to 29% for the full year from 25% in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA margin improved to 17% for the year from 15% in Q3. Net income increased significantly, from $8.8 million in Q3 to $68 million for the year.

  • The strategic focus shifted to scaling new products and leveraging AI for operational efficiency. Guidance for 2026 revenue growth was set at 19%–20%, higher than the high-teens initial view last quarter. The leadership transition to a new CEO was the most significant management change this quarter.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward the implications of new products and the impact of regulatory changes, whereas previous quarters were more concentrated on core business momentum and initial new product traction.

  • Management’s confidence was stronger, citing specific headwinds turning into tailwinds, such as the remittance tax and AI-driven efficiencies.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company acknowledged transaction loss rates may fluctuate quarter-to-quarter and highlighted ongoing macro and geopolitical uncertainties. Management plans to “manage costs thoughtfully” and invest in AI to mitigate operational risks. Analysts raised concerns about seasonality, the sustainability of high growth rates, and margin compression as new product investments ramp up.

Final Takeaway

Remitly concluded the call by reiterating its ambitions to transform cross-border financial services, supported by record revenue, expanding margins, and a robust product pipeline. With a strengthened leadership team, strategic investments in AI, and a clear path to scale new products, the company remains focused on accelerating growth, operational efficiency, and delivering on its vision to reach $3 billion in revenue and $600 million in adjusted EBITDA by 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: European indexes retreat after touching record levels
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.30% to 10,654.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.43% to 25,166. 

France (CAC:IND) -0.50% to 8,387. 

In other parts of Europe, Industrial production in Switzerland fell by 0.7% Y/Y in Q4.

Denmark’s consumer confidence index rose to -13.1 in February.

Switzerland’s trade surplus widened to CHF 3.6B in January.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) moved 0.24% lower to around €627.2, retreating slightly from record highs reached in the previous session. Traders analyze the outlook for European Central Bank policy, with recent data reinforcing expectations that rate cuts could begin later this year if inflation continues to moderate.

On the corporate side, Rio Tinto (RIO) led mining shares lower after reporting flat full-year profit; Nestle (NSRGY) climbed more than 3% after surpassing Q4 sales.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 2 basis points to 4.10%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 2.76%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 4.39%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)
